title: Mishandling of Exceptional Conditions

explain.what: When an error occurs during authentication or authorization, the code catches it and silently continues — granting access instead of denying it. This is called 'fail-open'.
explain.example: The token validation service throws an error for a malformed token. The catch block logs the error but never returns a denial response — so the server replies with 'Access granted' anyway.
explain.impact: Authentication bypass · unauthorized access · attackers deliberately trigger errors to gain entry

vulnerable.annotation: ⚠ The catch block handles the auth error but then falls through to the success response.

fixed.annotation: ✅ Any auth exception triggers an explicit denial. Fail closed — when in doubt, deny.

demoHint: Submit a short (invalid) token. Vulnerable grants access anyway. Fixed returns 503 and denies.
